scenario_results.reason: The lane resolved a supported visit-status request only after EHR transition, scheduling, CRM, plan-of-care frequency, and communication preference records aligned.
Missing patient, visit, address, payer, and requester-authority facts produced a blocked-state request for minimum prerequisites instead of disclosure or guesswork.
Refund, extra hours, and service-recovery exception requests were refused in-lane and packaged for the approval owner.
Eligibility was preserved as evidence but start-of-care scheduling stayed blocked because order, authorization, and referral documentation prerequisites were missing.
Unclear caregiver or representative authority triggered minimum-necessary handling and privacy escalation before disclosing visit notes or clinical records.
Clinical symptoms and treatment-plan questions were refused as customer-care work and routed to licensed clinical or urgent-response ownership.
The lane did not convert homebound or skilled-need facts into coverage, medical-necessity, or payer-policy conclusions and routed to utilization management or payer operations.
Coding, claim correction, denial, and appeal work was routed to revenue-cycle and coding specialists instead of remaining in customer care.
Repeat missed visits and unresolved callbacks were escalated as complaint and patient-safety-sensitive service failures with CRM, scheduling, EVV, and supervisor evidence.
Home health terminology such as SOC, recertification, OASIS-E2, HHA, ABN, 270/271, and plan-of-care frequency was preserved and mapped to source artifacts.
Conflicting payer portal, scheduling, referral, and tenant-policy records produced low-confidence escalation rather than direct clearance.
Tenant private-duty service-recovery thresholds were applied only with assumptions recorded and without weakening public healthcare, privacy, or payer boundaries.
A customer-care communication that exposed medication discrepancy and plan-of-care questions became a minimum-necessary handoff to licensed review.

notes: All 13 defined scenarios are represented in functionality-map.json scenario_coverage.
All required shared, service-support-and-resolution, and healthcare-and-care-delivery abilities are mapped and validated.
Functionality coverage is complete for all shared abilities, all service-support-and-resolution abilities, and all healthcare-and-care-delivery abilities.

The evaluation suite covers normal execution, missing facts, policy exceptions, repeat failures, coverage/referral/authorization prerequisites, PHI handling, clinical escalation, payer interpretation refusal, adjacent-lane routing, low-confidence conflict handling, terminology fidelity, and tenant adaptation.
Package remains explicit that runtime strategy is specification-based and does not claim retained implementation authority.
